2. Report Structure & Format.section_structure: 5/5 — all key sections present (4/4), 35 headings total

3. Content Completeness.technology_evolution: 5/5 — Covers the trajectory from concatenative and statistical parametric TTS to neural seq2seq/autoregressive models, then to 2020-era non-autoregressive (FastSpeech 2) and flow alignment (Glow-TTS), end-to-end neural vocoding integration (VITS), diffusion (Diff-TTS/Grad-TTS), and finally codec-token/large-scale pretraining approaches (VALL-E/NaturalSpeech 2/VALL-E 2). The narrative includes motivations and trade-offs, not just name-dropping.
3. Content Completeness.architecture_analysis: 5/5 — Analyzes multiple architecture families with principles, strengths/weaknesses, and representative works: autoregressive (incl. codec LM framing), non-autoregressive (FastSpeech 2), flow-based (Glow-TTS/Flowtron), diffusion-based (Diff-TTS/Grad-TTS/ProDiff, latent diffusion, bridges), plus explicit hybridization (SimpleSpeech 2, ControlSpeech). Meets the requirement of >=4 categories with technical discussion.
3. Content Completeness.technical_challenges: 5/5 — Discusses major challenges with substantive coverage: speed–quality trade-off, expressiveness/style control, zero-shot speaker cloning and generalization, multilingual/cross-lingual synthesis, data scale/annotation limits, and robustness/safety/misuse. Includes how current lines of work attempt to address them (variance modeling, controllability methods, pretraining/codec prompts, anti-spoofing/watermarking direction).
3. Content Completeness.evaluation_framework: 4/5 — Provides a structured subjective vs. objective evaluation discussion (MOS, preference/CMOS/AB, similarity MOS; MCD, F0 error, duration error, WER via ASR, speaker embedding similarity, ASV/ASVspoof). It explains limitations and emerging evaluation priorities. However, it omits several commonly cited objective metrics explicitly requested (e.g., PESQ, STOI, FAD) and does not deeply compare pros/cons across a broad standardized metric set.
3. Content Completeness.future_directions: 4/5 — Gives concrete, trend-grounded future directions (foundation models, disentanglement/controllability, real-time high-fidelity, multilingual/cross-cultural prosody, preference optimization/RLHF, safety/watermarking). The directions are reasonable and somewhat specific, but lack a clearer technical roadmap with short/medium/long-term staging or prioritization criteria.

4. Technical Depth.math_formulas: 0/5 — The report contains no explicit mathematical formulas, no algorithm pseudocode (e.g., attention equations, diffusion forward/reverse SDE/ODE, flow Jacobians, ELBO), and no computational complexity (Big-O) analysis.
4. Technical Depth.industry_cases: 1/5 — It focuses on academic papers (FastSpeech 2, VITS, VALL-E, etc.) rather than real-world industry TTS products/services. No concrete case studies of deployed systems (e.g., Google/Microsoft/NVIDIA/iFlytek/Baidu) with architecture diagrams, latency/RTF, scalability, or deployment metrics are provided.
4. Technical Depth.ethics_security: 2/5 — Ethics/safety is mentioned (deepfake risk, watermarking, provenance, ASVspoof 5) but remains high-level: no specific detection/watermarking methods, no quantitative security metrics (AUC/EER), no privacy/compliance discussion (GDPR/CCPA/DPIA), and no concrete implementation parameters (e.g., DP epsilon, FL design) or governance roadmap.
4. Technical Depth.quantitative_comparison: 1/5 — The narrative discusses trade-offs (quality vs speed, latency constraints) but provides no in-text tables or concrete numeric comparisons (MOS, RTF, parameter counts, steps, memory) and does not analyze quantitative root causes. Appendix references a CSV/table, but the report body contains no quantitative comparative analysis.

5. Academic Standards.IEEE_format: 3/3 — standard IEEE numbered citation format detected
5. Academic Standards.terminology_consistency: 4/4 — Technical terminology (e.g., autoregressive/non-autoregressive, flow-based, diffusion-based, neural codec language models, MOS, MCD, ASVspoof) is used accurately and consistently across sections. Key acronyms/metrics such as TTS and MOS are expanded on first appearance, and model family labels remain stable throughout. Minor areas could benefit from slightly more explicit first-use definitions (e.g., RLHF, CMOS/AB), but overall terminology control is strong; no figure/table numbering issues appear in the provided half (assets are referenced but not presented).
5. Academic Standards.writing_style: 4/4 — The style is predominantly academic: objective tone, clear structure, logical transitions, and mostly third-person framing. Colloquial language is minimal; occasional rhetorical phrasing (e.g., “In short,” “key takeaway is straightforward,” “winning architectures”) is somewhat report-like but still professional. Passive voice is not dominant, but the prose remains rigorous and suitable for an academic survey.

6. Data Analysis & Visualization.image_content_eval: 5/5 — Clear, high-quality visualization with a line chart (TTS research trends by year) and a grouped bar chart (architecture family comparison). Both are directly related to TTS, include descriptive titles, labeled axes, and complete legends for all metrics.

eval_report.comment: Good: Report is mostly complete, main content is well-covered, but some dimensions have room for improvement.
